svg.text: One Nuttall variant wins the first sidelobe. Another wins the far tail.

text.tspan: This pass splits the repo's old bare `nuttall` label into two explicit coefficient families at length 129. The minimum-4-term-Blackman-Harris
variant keeps the lowest first sidelobe. The continuous-derivative variant gives some of that back and buys cleaner far-out decay.
The lines show normalized offset response from 4 to 48 bins away from the tone.
The shape split is the real point: the minimum-4-term-BH variant keeps the first
sidelobe lowest, but the continuous variant falls away faster deeper into the
tail.
Best values are shaded. The continuous variant does
not win the first-sidelobe contest. It wins the far
tail.
The old repo `nuttall` is still a legitimate
window: its peak sidelobe lands at -96.83 dB,
deeper than Blackman-Harris (-92.04 dB) and deeper
than the continuous variant (-93.32 dB). If the job
is mostly “push the first sidelobe down,” the
minimum-4-term-BH branch still deserves the nod.
The deeper tail flips the story. In the 24–48 bin
band the minimum-4-term-BH variant only gets down
to -98.36 dB, while the continuous variant
reaches -117.35 dB. That is the real naming trap:
one family member wins the first sidelobe, the
other wins far-out decay.
So the bare name `nuttall` is not good enough once
the repo starts teaching sidelobe falloff. Keep
`nuttall` as a compatibility alias for the current
minimum-4-term-BH implementation, but surface both
variants explicitly in the new sidecar and in future
weak-spur-at-distance work.
